Derek Baker

Professional Biography

Structured. Driven.
Built to Adapt.

I'm Derek Baker — an accounting student at the University of Arizona's Eller College of Management, an Army veteran, and a member of the Colorado River Indian Tribes (CRIT). My path here runs through military service, a career in emergency services, and a single driving goal: to build skills and experience I can eventually bring back to my community.

My time as a Combat Medic trained me to be precise, detail-oriented, and composed under pressure. My years in emergency call reception and hospitality management sharpened that into something more — the ability to lead, instruct, and communicate across teams. I came into BCOM 314 to refine the professional persona that connects all of it.

Outside of academics, I'm building Excel-based RPG tools, precepting Korean conversation at the 300-level, and competing in a Korean language speaking contest this semester. I approach everything as a competition — and this professional journey is no different.

Adaptability Drive Precision Structure

The Evolution

* Replace placeholder values with your actual Self-Perception Inventory and Professional Reputation Survey scores.
Communication Growth: First vs. Third Presentation

The data tells the story directly. In the first presentation my individual contribution score was –1 — I showed up but wasn't communicatively present. By the third presentation that score rose to +7, matching the team's highest contributor. The aggregate scores held steady, meaning the team's floor didn't drop — my ceiling rose to meet it.

Framework
Conscious Competency
I moved from unconsciously silent in meetings — unaware of its impact — to consciously choosing to engage: affirming teammates, reiterating points. Team confidence measurably improved once I made that shift.
Framework
Intention vs. Impact
My intention was always to listen carefully. The impact read as cold and disengaged. Closing that gap required behavioral change — not just awareness. Fully showing up to the final project moved both the grade and the team.
Framework
Growth Mindset
Peer feedback confirmed what my survey data suggested: I need to build the skill of giving feedback in-the-moment, not just at the end. That's the next frontier — one I'm actively working on in upper-division coursework.
01
Core Values: Adaptability & Drive
These values show up directly in how I describe myself — well-rounded, well-informed, perfectionist. My military background reinforced both: I don't wait to be assigned a task I know I can handle. Survey respondents confirmed hardworking and self-confident, meaning this identity-to-reputation alignment is one of my strongest areas.
02
Communication Style: Analytical Driver
The Army built a task-first communication style — efficient, precise, and easy to misread as cold. My Self-Perception Inventory placed me as Analytical/Driver. Survey data confirmed this. The key insight: in collaborative settings, warmth must be intentional. It doesn't happen by default for me, which means I have to schedule it.
03
Team Culture: Structure as Foundation
I thrive in hierarchical, deadline-driven environments. My team operated loosely, which created early friction. What I learned: when structure doesn't exist, you can create it. Volunteering to set objectives — rather than waiting for someone to assign them — is itself a leadership behavior I hadn't previously recognized as such.
04
The Meeting Data Point
For earlier projects I was present but not communicatively active. Team confidence was lower and grades reflected that gap. For the final project I attended every meeting fully — affirming, reiterating, contributing. The grade was notably higher and teammates reported feeling more confident. The data made the case I hadn't made with words.
Portfolio
Evidence · Skills · Context
Work that earns its place here.
Each sample below was selected because it demonstrates a specific, transferable skill relevant to public accounting. For each piece I provide the context it was created in, the claim it supports about my professional capabilities, and the reasoning behind its inclusion for an audience at EY and similar firms.
Solo Work
Data · Excel · Systems Architecture
TMNT RPG Character Generator
Context: Built independently for the Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles tabletop RPG system — for entertainment and to actively strengthen Excel proficiency outside coursework.

The workbook spans three sheets — Character Stats Generation, Species Generation, and Mutation Generation — using RANDBETWEEN for dice rolls, nested IF logic for conditional stat bonuses, VLOOKUP tables for descriptors, and array formulas to dynamically calculate derived values like hit points, carry weight, and movement speed. Every stat is generated, evaluated, and labeled automatically with zero manual input.
Advanced Excel Data Validation Conditional Logic Self-Directed
Solo Work
Python · Machine Learning · Data Analysis
Drug Enforcement Prediction Model
Context: Built in a university data analytics course using Python in Google Colab. The dataset tracked monthly drug volumes entering the U.S., alongside variables like source country risk level, enforcement intensity, and U.S. demand.

What it does: The project answers a practical question — can you predict how intensely authorities will respond to drug trafficking based on volume and demand data? The analysis moves through data cleaning, grouping by risk level and enforcement intensity, and a bar chart visualization, before culminating in a trained XGBoost machine learning model that classifies predicted enforcement intensity for any given demand and volume scenario.

What it communicates: Higher-risk source countries consistently produced greater drug volumes entering the U.S. — and monthly drug volume proved to be the stronger predictor of enforcement response, outperforming demand alone. The model takes new input values and outputs a probability score across all enforcement intensity levels — at US Demand: 30 and Monthly Volume: 400,000, it returned a 99.82% probability of Minimum enforcement intensity.
Python / Pandas XGBoost / PyCaret Data Visualization Predictive Modeling
Group Work — Lead Author & Modeling Advisor
Graduation Rate Regression Analysis
U.S. Department of Education · 1,513 Universities · 12 Models Tested
Regression Tableau Lead Author
Page 1 of 11
Context

A team project analyzing a U.S. Department of Education dataset covering 1,513 universities to identify which institutional factors statistically predict 6-year bachelor's degree graduation rates.

My Contribution

Primary author of all written content and formatting decisions. Advised the team on all 12 regression models — guiding variable selection, log transformations to correct heteroskedasticity, and interpretation of significant variables.

What It Found

Final model: R² of 96.94%. Grant aid volume and admission rate negatively predict graduation rate. Tuition, religious affiliation, and larger enrollment positively predict it. Rural location was the only urbanization category with a significant negative effect.

12 Models Tested R² = 96.94% Lead Author Regression Modeling Tableau
Solo Work — Financial Analysis
Hasbro 10-K Analysis
Annual Report Filed 2-27-2025 · Income Statement · Ratio Analysis · Revenue Trends
Accounting 10-K Solo
Page 1 of 8
Context

A solo financial analysis of Hasbro's 10-K annual report filed February 2025, examining income statement structure, comprehensive income, PP&E depreciation methods, revenue recognition, and three-year financial trends.

What It Demonstrates

Ability to read and interpret a real-world 10-K filing — classifying income statement format, locating accounting policy footnotes, calculating profit margin, current ratio, and debt-to-equity ratio, and explaining what the numbers mean in plain language.

Key Findings

Net income dropped 832.9% from 2022–2023 while revenue fell only 14.6% — driven by the sale of the entertainment division to Lionsgate and goodwill write-downs, not core business weakness. Cost-cutting in 2024 restored profitability despite continued revenue decline.

10-K Filing Analysis Current Ratio Debt-to-Equity Vertical Analysis Revenue Trend
